rad_findings_en:
Solid subpleural 6 mm nodule (volume 49 mm3) in the right lower lobe (series 4, image 162), unchanged.

Solid 4 mm nodule in the right upper lobe (series 4, image 93), unchanged.

Few solid nodules measuring up to 3 mm in both lungs, unchanged.

Isolated calcifications in both lungs (clinically insignificant).

Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula, and basal segments bilaterally (clinically insignificant).

Areas of reticular opacity paravertebral in the right lower lobe, likely fibrotic changes (clinically insignificant).

No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Single enlarged 4R lymph node measuring up to 11 mm in short axis, with preserved morphology.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.

No clinically significant pericardial effusion.

Moderate coronary artery calcification.

Calcification of the aortic, mitral, and tricuspid valves.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification.

Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Decreased height of the T11 vertebral body by 28.0%.

Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T12 - 112 HU, L1 - 124 HU.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
rad_impression_en:
1. Solid subpleural 6 mm nodule (volume 49 mm3) in the right lower lobe, solid 4 mm nodule in the right upper lobe, and few solid nodules measuring up to 3 mm in both lungs, unchanged compared with June 05, 2025. In accordance with 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, additional CT follow-up is not recommended.

2. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

3. Mild pulmonary emphysema.

4. Thoracic aortic calcification.

5. Calcification of the aortic, mitral, and tricuspid valves.

6. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: In the lung parenchyma, solid nodules are identified, in series 4 presented as follows:
- Solid subpleural nodule measures 6 mm in the right lower lobe (image 162), unchanged.
- Solid nodule measures 4 mm in the right upper lobe (image 93), unchanged.
Few solid nodules measure up to 3 mm in both lungs, unchanged.
Isolated calcifications in both lungs (clinically insignificant).

Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula, and basal segments bilaterally (clinically insignificant).

Areas of reticular opacity paravertebral in the right lower lobe, likely fibrotic changes (clinically insignificant).

No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Single enlarged 4R lymph node measuring up to 11 mm in short axis, with preserved morphology.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcifications in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, right coronary artery. Calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T12 - 112 HU, L1 - 124 HU.

Upper abdomen: there is a subcapsular lesion in the right kidney 1.9 × 1.8 cm, 20 HU (previously 1.6 × 1.3 cm, 23 HU).
val_impression_en:
1. Solid subpleural 6 mm nodule (volume 49 mm3) in the right lower lobe, solid 4 mm nodule in the right upper lobe, and few solid nodules measuring up to 3 mm in both lungs, unchanged compared with June 05, 2025. In accordance with 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, additional CT follow-up is not recommended.

2. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

3. Mild pulmonary emphysema.

4. Thoracic aortic calcification.

5. Calcification of the aortic, mitral, and tricuspid valves.

6. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (predominantly in the upper lobes). The left hemidiaphragm is mildly elevated; on this background, the lower-basal portions of the left lung are compressed, with linear subsegmental atelectasis.

Linear atelectasis is also present in the right middle and lower lobes and in the left lingular segments.

In the lung parenchyma, single solid nodules are identified, in series 5 presented as follows:
No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the expanded portions of the lungs.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and central bronchi (except for the atelectasis described above) are patent.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta measures up to 4.1 cm, with wall calcification and S-shaped tortuosity of the descending segment. The main pulmonary artery measures up to 4.5 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level of less than 100 HU (CT signs of osteoporosis). Mild rightward scoliosis of the thoracic spine with apex at the T10 level.

Included abdomen:
In the liver parenchyma, hypodense lesions with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density are present, the largest in segment 7 measuring up to 3.0 × 2.4 cm. Pancreatic lipomatosis.

Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, an indistinct heterogeneous hypodense lesion measures up to 1.1 × 0.9 cm; previously the visualization was unsatisfactory.
rad_impression_en:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the expanded portions of the lungs. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (4.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis, aneurysmal dilatation, and S-shaped tortuosity of the thoracic aorta.

6. Cardiomegaly.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis at the T11-L1 level. Mild rightward scoliosis.

8. Hypodense liver lesions: Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, no CT follow-up is required.

9. Thyroid nodules: Per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (predominantly in the upper lobes). The left hemidiaphragm is mildly elevated; on this background, the lower-basal portions of the left lung are compressed, with linear subsegmental atelectasis.

Linear atelectasis is also present in the right middle and lower lobes and in the left lingular segments.

In the lung parenchyma, single solid nodules are identified, in series 5 presented as follows:
No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the expanded portions of the lungs.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and central bronchi (except for the atelectasis described above) are patent.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta measures up to 4.1 cm, with wall calcification and S-shaped tortuosity of the descending segment. The main pulmonary artery measures up to 4.5 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation,  diffuse ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament. Mild rightward scoliosis of the thoracic spine with apex at the T10 level. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 60 HU, T12 - 59 HU, L1 - 63 HU. Osteosclerotic lesion of T4 vertebral body

Included abdomen:
In the liver parenchyma, hypodense lesions with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density are present, the largest in segment 7 measuring up to 3.0 × 2.4 cm, unchanged. Pancreatic lipomatosis.

Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, an hypodense lesion measures up to 1.1 × 0.9 cm; previously the visualization was unsatisfactory.
val_impression_en:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the expanded portions of the lungs. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (4.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis, aneurysmal dilatation, and S-shaped tortuosity of the thoracic aorta.

6. Cardiomegaly.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis at the T11-L1 level. Mild rightward scoliosis.

8. Multiple hepatic cysts. Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, no CT follow-up is required.

9. Thyroid nodules: Per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: In the lingular segments and left lower lobe, polymorphous areas of subpleural and peribronchial ground-glass opacity. In the supradiaphragmatic portions of the left lung, a few bronchiolectases. In lung bases of the left upper lobe, bronchiectasis with areas of "air trapping". Left interlobular septa are irregularly thickened. At the level of the left upper and lower lobes, small subpleural parenchymal bands.  
Pulmonary nodules in series 5:  
Right middle lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 98.  
Right lower lobe:  
- Solid nodule along the interlobar pleura measuring 3 mm on image 105.  
Pleural effusion: Mild volume on the left, with layering thickness up to 4 mm.

Mediastinum and lung hila: In the fat of the middle mediastinum, left paratracheal, a partially calcified conglomerate without clear margins is seen, likely involving lymph nodes; the lumen of the left upper lobe bronchus is compressed and irregularly narrowed. Left perihilar and subcarinal lymph nodes with calcifications. A diverticulum of the posterior tracheal wall is present at the level of the inferior pole of the right thyroid lobe, measuring 10 × 8 mm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Unremarkable.

Upper abdomen: Small calcifications in the splenic parenchyma. A lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measures 1.2 cm with density -5 HU.

Thyroid gland: In the parenchyma of the left thyroid lobe at the superior scan margin, a calcified nodule measures 5 mm.
rad_impression_en:
1. Ground-glass opacities in the left upper and lower lobes, as part of inflammatory changes, likely in the setting of a chronic interstitial fibrosing process with formation of traction bronchiectasis and bronchiolectasis.

2. Partially calcified conglomerate in the middle mediastinal fat with narrowing of the lumen of the left upper lobe bronchus. Calcification of mediastinal and left perihilar lymph nodes.

3. Solid nodules in the right lung measuring less than 6 mm. Per 2017 Fleischner Society guidelines, no additional CT follow-up is recommended.

4. Mild left pleural effusion.

5. Calcified nodule in the left thyroid lobe parenchyma at the superior scan margin.

6. Calcifications in the splenic parenchyma, likely postinflammatory granulomas.

7. Lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland, most likely an adenoma. CT follow-up in 6–12 months.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: The volume of the left lung is smaller than the right one. In the left lung there is diffuse peripheral thickening of the interlobular interstitium and multiple ground-glass opacities, more pronounced in the lower parts, with the formation of small subpleural areas of consolidation. In the right lung there is no consolidation or ground-glass opacities.
Solid nodules are identified in series 5, presented as follows:
Right middle lobe:  
- Nodule measures 4 mm on image 98, may be a plug in the lumen of the bronchus.
Right lower lobe:  
- Solid nodule along the interlobar pleura measuring 3 mm on image 105, perifissural node, requires no follow-up.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: The mediastinum is shifted to the left. In the middle mediastinum, left paratracheal, a partially calcified mass without well-defined margins is seen; the lumen of the left upper lobe bronchus is compressed and irregularly narrowed. Left perihilar and subcarinal lymph nodes are enlarged, with calcifications. The exact size of the mass and its relationship to the left pulmonary artery without contrast is difficult to determine. A diverticulum of the posterior tracheal wall is present at the level of the inferior pole of the right thyroid lobe, measuring 10 × 8 mm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Subchondral cysts of the right shoulder joint (degenerative changes).

Upper abdomen: Small calcifications in the splenic parenchyma, likely post-inflammatory granulomas, no need for further follow-up. A lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measures 1.2 cm with density -5 HU.

Thyroid gland: In the parenchyma of the left thyroid lobe at the superior scan margin, a calcified nodule measures 5 mm. According to ACR White Paper recommendations no need for further follow-up.
val_impression_en:
1. Partially calcified mass in the middle mediastinum with narrowing of the lumen of the left upper lobe bronchus, calcification and enlargement of left perihilar and subcarinal lymph nodes may represent fibrosing mediastinitis. Interlobular septal thickening and ground-glass opacities in the left lung may be secondary to compressed veno-lymphatic pathways. 

2. Two nodules in the right lung, in the right middle lobe likely a plug in the lumen of the bronchus, in the right lower lobe likely perifissural node, measuring up to 4 mm. Per 2017 Fleischner Society guidelines, no additional CT follow-up is recommended.

3. Lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland, most likely an adenoma. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura:
Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and in lung bases bilaterally.
Areas of pulmonary emphysema (more on the right). 11.
In the left upper lobe (series 5), a ground-glass nodule up to 1.1 × 1.1 cm (image 125) persists, with no interval change on independent repeat measurement.
A few perifissural solid nodule on the right up to 3 mm.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart:
No cardiomegaly.
No clinically significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification.
Mild coronary calcification (left coronary artery, left anterior descending artery). The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels:
The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification.
The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 35 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11 level to +148 HU.

Upper abdomen:
In the splenic parenchyma (inferior portions), a hypodense lesion with ill-defined margins measures up to 1.4 × 1.2 cm with density of approximately +35 HU.
Areas of fatty replacement of the pancreas.
rad_impression_en:
1. Solitary ground-glass nodule in the left upper lobe (per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, CT follow-up per scheme: CT in 6–12 months to confirm persistence of the nodule, then CT every 2 years until 5 years). Pulmonary emphysema. Perifissural solid nodule on the right.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Low bone density at the T11 level.

6. Hypodense splenic lesion with ill-defined margins (correlate with MRI findings). Pancreatic lipomatosis.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple linear atelectasis in lung bases bilaterally, a few atelectasis in the right middle lobe, left lingula. A few small cysts in the lower lobe of the right lung, unchanged, clinically insignificant. In the left upper lobe a ground-glass nodule up to 10 × 7 mm (series 6, image 251), previously on November 6, 2025, 10 × 6 mm, stable compared to October 28, 2024. A few stable perifissural nodules (lymph nodes) on the right up to 3 mm, require no follow-up. No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: The heart is slightly enlarged due to increased size of the chambers. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Aortic valve calcification. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 35 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Bone islands of T12, T6 vertebral bodies, unchanged.

Upper abdomen: In the splenic parenchyma (inferior portions), a hypodense lesion with ill-defined margins measures up to 1.4 × 1.2 cm with density of approximately +21 HU, unchanged compared to October 28, 2024. For stable splenic mass with nondiagnostic features, ACR White Paper recommendations (Heller, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2013;10:833-839) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.
val_impression_en:
1. Solitary ground-glass nodule in the left upper lobe, stable compared to November 6, 2025 and October 28, 2024. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Chest CT every 2 years, until 5 years after the initial discovery of the nodule.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: A few calcifications in the left upper lobe, measuring 6 mm on image 50; measuring 4 mm. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified. Aeration is unevenly increased due to multiple areas of mixed emphysema with a total extent of involvement of 17%. Subsegmental atelectasis in the upper and lower lobes of both lungs. No pleural effusion. Bochdalek hernias, measuring 5.4 × 2.0 cm on the right and 3.8 × 1.6 cm on the left.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcium is not identified. Calcifications are present in the walls of the coronary arteries: Agatston score 47, CAC-DRS A1. Increased paracardiac fat volume is identified: total volume 218 mL, epicardial 151 mL, pericardial 67 mL.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 134 HU, T12 - 122 HU, L1 - 112 HU. Severe degenerative-dystrophic changes of the spine with formation of marginal osteophytes in the C6-L2 segment.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
rad_impression_en:
1. CT findings of mixed pulmonary emphysema (total extent of involvement about 17%). Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria.

2. A few calcified nodules in the left upper lobe, consistent with postinflammatory changes. These changes do not require follow-up.

3. Subsegmental atelectasis in the upper and lower lobes of both lungs.

4. Mild coronary calcification (Agatston 47, CAC-DRS A1). The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Bilateral Bochdalek hernias.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization). Clinical correlation is recommended and consideration of bone densitometry.

7. Severe degenerative-dystrophic changes of the spine.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Severe confluent centrilobular emphysema. In the upper lobe of the left lung (series 5, images 47-52), calcified nodules measure 4 mm and 3 mm. On the right, the interlobar perifissural nodule measures up to 3 mm, lymph node, requires no follow-up. In the right lower lobe, a subpleural nodule measures 5 mm (image 179). In the left lower lobe, a subpleural nodule measures 3 mm (image 196). Discoid atelectasis in lung bases, middle lobe and lingular segments. No pleural effusion. Bochdalek hernias, measuring 5.4 × 2.0 cm on the right and 3.8 × 1.6 cm on the left.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcifications. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 134 HU, T12 - 122 HU, L1 - 112 HU. Moderate degenerative changes of the spine.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression_en:
1. Calcified nodules in the left upper lobe measure up to 4 mm, healed granulomas, require no follow-up. Subpleural nodules measure up to 5 mm in lower lobes. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Severe confluent centrilobular emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
